sourceRefs.fallbackText: pathlib — Object-oriented filesystem paths
sourceRefs.textQuote: pathlib — Object-oriented filesystem paths

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Theme Auto Light Dark |
sourceRefs.textQuote: Theme Auto Light Dark |

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.4.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.4.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Source code: Lib/pathlib/
sourceRefs.textQuote: Source code: Lib/pathlib/

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This module offers classes representing filesystem paths with semantics appropriate for different operating systems. Path classes are divided between pure paths , which provide purely computational operations without I/O, and concrete paths , which inherit from pure paths but also provide I/O operations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This module offers classes representing filesystem paths with semantics appropriate for different operating systems. Path classes are divided between pure paths , which provide purely computational operations without I/O

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Image metadata: Inheritance diagram showing the classes available in pathlib. The most basic class is PurePath, which has three direct subclasses: PurePosixPath, PureWindowsPath, and Path. Further to these four classes, there are two classes that use multiple inheritance: PosixPath subclasses PurePosixPath and Path, and WindowsPath subclasses PureWindowsPath
sourceRefs.textQuote: Image metadata: Inheritance diagram showing the classes available in pathlib. The most basic class is PurePath, which has three direct subclasses: PurePosixPath, PureWindowsPath, and Path. Further to these four classes,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you’ve never used this module before or just aren’t sure which class is right for your task, Path is most likely what you need. It instantiates a concrete path for the platform the code is running on.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you’ve never used this module before or just aren’t sure which class is right for your task, Path is most likely what you need. It instantiates a concrete path for the platform the code is running on.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pure paths are useful in some special cases; for example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pure paths are useful in some special cases; for example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you want to manipulate Windows paths on a Unix machine (or vice versa). You cannot instantiate a WindowsPath when running on Unix, but you can instantiate PureWindowsPath .
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you want to manipulate Windows paths on a Unix machine (or vice versa). You cannot instantiate a WindowsPath when running on Unix, but you can instantiate PureWindowsPath .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You want to make sure that your code only manipulates paths without actually accessing the OS. In this case, instantiating one of the pure classes may be useful since those simply don’t have any OS-accessing operations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: You want to make sure that your code only manipulates paths without actually accessing the OS. In this case, instantiating one of the pure classes may be useful since those simply don’t have any OS-accessing operations.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: PEP 428 : The pathlib module – object-oriented filesystem paths.
sourceRefs.textQuote: PEP 428 : The pathlib module – object-oriented filesystem paths.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For low-level path manipulation on strings, you can also use the os.path module.
sourceRefs.textQuote: For low-level path manipulation on strings, you can also use the os.path module.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Importing the main class:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Importing the main class:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> from pathlib import Path
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> from pathlib import Path

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Listing subdirectories:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Listing subdirectories:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = Path ( '.' ) >>> [ x for x in p . iterdir () if x . is_dir ()] [PosixPath('.hg'), PosixPath('docs'), PosixPath('dist'), PosixPath('__pycache__'), PosixPath('build')]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = Path ( '.' ) >>> [ x for x in p . iterdir () if x . is_dir ()] [PosixPath('.hg'), PosixPath('docs'), PosixPath('dist'), PosixPath('__pycache__'), PosixPath('build')]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Listing Python source files in this directory tree:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Listing Python source files in this directory tree:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> list ( p . glob ( '**/*.py' )) [PosixPath('test_pathlib.py'), PosixPath('setup.py'), PosixPath('pathlib.py'), PosixPath('docs/conf.py'), PosixPath('build/lib/pathlib.py')]
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> list ( p . glob ( '**/*.py' )) [PosixPath('test_pathlib.py'), PosixPath('setup.py'), PosixPath('pathlib.py'), PosixPath('docs/conf.py'), PosixPath('build/lib/pathlib.py')]

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Navigating inside a directory tree:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Navigating inside a directory tree:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = Path ( '/etc' ) >>> q = p / 'init.d' / 'reboot' >>> q PosixPath('/etc/init.d/reboot') >>> q . resolve () PosixPath('/etc/rc.d/init.d/halt')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = Path ( '/etc' ) >>> q = p / 'init.d' / 'reboot' >>> q PosixPath('/etc/init.d/reboot') >>> q . resolve () PosixPath('/etc/rc.d/init.d/halt')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Querying path properties:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Querying path properties:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> q . exists () True >>> q . is_dir () False
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> q . exists () True >>> q . is_dir () False

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> with q . open () as f : f . readline () ... '#!/bin/bash\n'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> with q . open () as f : f . readline () ... '#!/bin/bash\n'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An exception inheriting NotImplementedError that is raised when an unsupported operation is called on a path object.
sourceRefs.textQuote: An exception inheriting NotImplementedError that is raised when an unsupported operation is called on a path object.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.13.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.13.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pure path objects provide path-handling operations which don’t actually access a filesystem. There are three ways to access these classes, which we also call flavours :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pure path objects provide path-handling operations which don’t actually access a filesystem. There are three ways to access these classes, which we also call flavours :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A generic class that represents the system’s path flavour (instantiating it creates either a PurePosixPath or a PureWindowsPath ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: A generic class that represents the system’s path flavour (instantiating it creates either a PurePosixPath or a PureWindowsPath ):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePath ( 'setup.py' ) # Running on a Unix machine PurePosixPath('setup.py')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePath ( 'setup.py' ) # Running on a Unix machine PurePosixPath('setup.py')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Each element of pathsegments can be either a string representing a path segment, or an object implementing the os.PathLike interface where the __fspath__() method returns a string, such as another path object:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Each element of pathsegments can be either a string representing a path segment, or an object implementing the os.PathLike interface where the __fspath__() method returns a string, such as another path object:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePath ( 'foo' , 'some/path' , 'bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/some/path/bar') >>> PurePath ( Path ( 'foo' ), Path ( 'bar' )) PurePosixPath('foo/bar')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePath ( 'foo' , 'some/path' , 'bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/some/path/bar') >>> PurePath ( Path ( 'foo' ), Path ( 'bar' )) PurePosixPath('foo/bar')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When pathsegments is empty, the current directory is assumed:
sourceRefs.textQuote: When pathsegments is empty, the current directory is assumed:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePath () PurePosixPath('.')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePath () PurePosixPath('.')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If a segment is an absolute path, all previous segments are ignored (like os.path.join() ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: If a segment is an absolute path, all previous segments are ignored (like os.path.join() ):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePath ( '/etc' , '/usr' , 'lib64' ) PurePosixPath('/usr/lib64') >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Windows' , 'd:bar' ) PureWindowsPath('d:bar')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePath ( '/etc' , '/usr' , 'lib64' ) PurePosixPath('/usr/lib64') >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Windows' , 'd:bar' ) PureWindowsPath('d:bar')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: On Windows, the drive is not reset when a rooted relative path segment (e.g., r'\foo' ) is encountered:
sourceRefs.textQuote: On Windows, the drive is not reset when a rooted relative path segment (e.g., r'\foo' ) is encountered:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Windows' , '/Program Files' ) PureWindowsPath('c:/Program Files')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Windows' , '/Program Files' ) PureWindowsPath('c:/Program Files')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Spurious slashes and single dots are collapsed, but double dots ( '..' ) and leading double slashes ( '//' ) are not, since this would change the meaning of a path for various reasons (e.g. symbolic links, UNC paths):
sourceRefs.textQuote: Spurious slashes and single dots are collapsed, but double dots ( '..' ) and leading double slashes ( '//' ) are not, since this would change the meaning of a path for various reasons (e.g. symbolic links, UNC paths):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePath ( 'foo//bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( '//foo/bar' ) PurePosixPath('//foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( 'foo/./bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( 'foo/../bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/../bar')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePath ( 'foo//bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( '//foo/bar' ) PurePosixPath('//foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( 'foo/./bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/bar') >>> PurePath ( 'foo/../bar' ) PurePosixPath('foo/../bar')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (a naïve approach would make PurePosixPath('foo/../bar') equivalent to PurePosixPath('bar') , which is wrong if foo is a symbolic link to another directory)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (a naïve approach would make PurePosixPath('foo/../bar') equivalent to PurePosixPath('bar') , which is wrong if foo is a symbolic link to another directory)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pure path objects implement the os.PathLike interface, allowing them to be used anywhere the interface is accepted.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pure path objects implement the os.PathLike interface, allowing them to be used anywhere the interface is accepted.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.6: Added support for the os.PathLike interface.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.6: Added support for the os.PathLike interface.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A subclass of PurePath , this path flavour represents non-Windows filesystem paths:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A subclass of PurePath , this path flavour represents non-Windows filesystem paths:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc/hosts' ) PurePosixPath('/etc/hosts')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc/hosts' ) PurePosixPath('/etc/hosts')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: pathsegments is specified similarly to PurePath .
sourceRefs.textQuote: pathsegments is specified similarly to PurePath .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A subclass of PurePath , this path flavour represents Windows filesystem paths, including UNC paths :
sourceRefs.textQuote: A subclass of PurePath , this path flavour represents Windows filesystem paths, including UNC paths :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/' , 'Users' , 'Ximénez' ) PureWindowsPath('c:/Users/Ximénez') >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//server/share/file' ) PureWindowsPath('//server/share/file')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/' , 'Users' , 'Ximénez' ) PureWindowsPath('c:/Users/Ximénez') >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//server/share/file' ) PureWindowsPath('//server/share/file')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: pathsegments is specified similarly to PurePath .
sourceRefs.textQuote: pathsegments is specified similarly to PurePath .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Regardless of the system you’re running on, you can instantiate all of these classes, since they don’t provide any operation that does system calls.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Regardless of the system you’re running on, you can instantiate all of these classes, since they don’t provide any operation that does system calls.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Paths are immutable and hashable . Paths of a same flavour are comparable and orderable. These properties respect the flavour’s case-folding semantics:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Paths are immutable and hashable . Paths of a same flavour are comparable and orderable. These properties respect the flavour’s case-folding semantics:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) == PurePosixPath ( 'FOO' ) False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) == PureWindowsPath ( 'FOO' ) True >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'FOO' ) in { PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) } True >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'C:' ) < PureWindowsPath ( 'd:' ) True
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) == PurePosixPath ( 'FOO' ) False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) == PureWindowsPath ( 'FOO' ) True >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'FOO' ) in { PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) } True >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'C:'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Paths of a different flavour compare unequal and cannot be ordered:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Paths of a different flavour compare unequal and cannot be ordered:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) == PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) < PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> TypeError : '<' not supported between instances of 'PureWindowsPath' and 'PurePosixPath'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) == PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'foo' ) < PurePosixPath ( 'foo' ) Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>" , line 1 , in <module> TypeError : '<' not supporte

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The slash operator helps create child paths, like os.path.join() . If the argument is an absolute path, the previous path is ignored. On Windows, the drive is not reset when the argument is a rooted relative path (e.g., r'\foo' ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: The slash operator helps create child paths, like os.path.join() . If the argument is an absolute path, the previous path is ignored. On Windows, the drive is not reset when the argument is a rooted relative path (e.g.,

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> p PurePosixPath('/etc') >>> p / 'init.d' / 'apache2' PurePosixPath('/etc/init.d/apache2') >>> q = PurePath ( 'bin' ) >>> '/usr' / q PurePosixPath('/usr/bin') >>> p / '/an_absolute_path' PurePosixPath('/an_absolute_path') >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Windows' , '/Program Files' ) PureWindowsPath('c:/Program Files')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> p PurePosixPath('/etc') >>> p / 'init.d' / 'apache2' PurePosixPath('/etc/init.d/apache2') >>> q = PurePath ( 'bin' ) >>> '/usr' / q PurePosixPath('/usr/bin') >>> p / '/an_absolute_path' Pu

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A path object can be used anywhere an object implementing os.PathLike is accepted:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A path object can be used anywhere an object implementing os.PathLike is accepted:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> import os >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> os . fspath ( p ) '/etc'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> import os >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> os . fspath ( p ) '/etc'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The string representation of a path is the raw filesystem path itself (in native form, e.g. with backslashes under Windows), which you can pass to any function taking a file path as a string:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The string representation of a path is the raw filesystem path itself (in native form, e.g. with backslashes under Windows), which you can pass to any function taking a file path as a string:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> str ( p ) '/etc' >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files' ) >>> str ( p ) 'c:\\Program Files'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc' ) >>> str ( p ) '/etc' >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files' ) >>> str ( p ) 'c:\\Program Files'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Similarly, calling bytes on a path gives the raw filesystem path as a bytes object, as encoded by os.fsencode() :
sourceRefs.textQuote: Similarly, calling bytes on a path gives the raw filesystem path as a bytes object, as encoded by os.fsencode() :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> bytes ( p ) b'/etc'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> bytes ( p ) b'/etc'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Calling bytes is only recommended under Unix. Under Windows, the unicode form is the canonical representation of filesystem paths.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Calling bytes is only recommended under Unix. Under Windows, the unicode form is the canonical representation of filesystem paths.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To access the individual “parts” (components) of a path, use the following property:
sourceRefs.textQuote: To access the individual “parts” (components) of a path, use the following property:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A tuple giving access to the path’s various components:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A tuple giving access to the path’s various components:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePath ( '/usr/bin/python3' ) >>> p . parts ('/', 'usr', 'bin', 'python3') >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/PSF' ) >>> p . parts ('c:\\', 'Program Files', 'PSF')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePath ( '/usr/bin/python3' ) >>> p . parts ('/', 'usr', 'bin', 'python3') >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/PSF' ) >>> p . parts ('c:\\', 'Program Files', 'PSF')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (note how the drive and local root are regrouped in a single part)
sourceRefs.textQuote: (note how the drive and local root are regrouped in a single part)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Pure paths provide the following methods and properties:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Pure paths provide the following methods and properties:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The implementation of the os.path module used for low-level path parsing and joining: either posixpath or ntpath .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The implementation of the os.path module used for low-level path parsing and joining: either posixpath or ntpath .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Added in version 3.13.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.13.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A string representing the drive letter or name, if any:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A string representing the drive letter or name, if any:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . drive 'c:' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '/Program Files/' ) . drive '' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . drive ''
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . drive 'c:' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '/Program Files/' ) . drive '' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . drive ''

sourceRefs.fallbackText: UNC shares are also considered drives:
sourceRefs.textQuote: UNC shares are also considered drives:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share/foo.txt' ) . drive '\\\\host\\share'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share/foo.txt' ) . drive '\\\\host\\share'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A string representing the (local or global) root, if any:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A string representing the (local or global) root, if any:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . root '\\' >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:Program Files/' ) . root '' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . root '/'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . root '\\' >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:Program Files/' ) . root '' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . root '/'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: UNC shares always have a root:
sourceRefs.textQuote: UNC shares always have a root:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share' ) . root '\\'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share' ) . root '\\'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If the path starts with more than two successive slashes, PurePosixPath collapses them:
sourceRefs.textQuote: If the path starts with more than two successive slashes, PurePosixPath collapses them:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( '//etc' ) . root '//' >>> PurePosixPath ( '///etc' ) . root '/' >>> PurePosixPath ( '////etc' ) . root '/'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( '//etc' ) . root '//' >>> PurePosixPath ( '///etc' ) . root '/' >>> PurePosixPath ( '////etc' ) . root '/'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This behavior conforms to The Open Group Base Specifications Issue 6 , paragraph 4.11 Pathname Resolution :
sourceRefs.textQuote: This behavior conforms to The Open Group Base Specifications Issue 6 , paragraph 4.11 Pathname Resolution :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: “A pathname that begins with two successive slashes may be interpreted in an implementation-defined manner, although more than two leading slashes shall be treated as a single slash.”
sourceRefs.textQuote: “A pathname that begins with two successive slashes may be interpreted in an implementation-defined manner, although more than two leading slashes shall be treated as a single slash.”

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The concatenation of the drive and root:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The concatenation of the drive and root:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . anchor 'c:\\' >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:Program Files/' ) . anchor 'c:' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . anchor '/' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share' ) . anchor '\\\\host\\share\\'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/Program Files/' ) . anchor 'c:\\' >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:Program Files/' ) . anchor 'c:' >>> PurePosixPath ( '/etc' ) . anchor '/' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//host/share' ) . anchor '\\\\host\\sh

sourceRefs.fallbackText: An immutable sequence providing access to the logical ancestors of the path:
sourceRefs.textQuote: An immutable sequence providing access to the logical ancestors of the path:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/foo/bar/setup.py' ) >>> p . parents [ 0 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/foo/bar') >>> p . parents [ 1 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/foo') >>> p . parents [ 2 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/foo/bar/setup.py' ) >>> p . parents [ 0 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/foo/bar') >>> p . parents [ 1 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/foo') >>> p . parents [ 2 ] PureWindowsPath('c:/')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.10: The parents sequence now supports slices and negative index values.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.10: The parents sequence now supports slices and negative index values.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The logical parent of the path:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The logical parent of the path:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '/a/b/c/d' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('/a/b/c')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '/a/b/c/d' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('/a/b/c')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: You cannot go past an anchor, or empty path:
sourceRefs.textQuote: You cannot go past an anchor, or empty path:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '/' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('/') >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '.' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('.')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '/' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('/') >>> p = PurePosixPath ( '.' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('.')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is a purely lexical operation, hence the following behaviour:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This is a purely lexical operation, hence the following behaviour:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( 'foo/..' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('foo')
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePosixPath ( 'foo/..' ) >>> p . parent PurePosixPath('foo')

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If you want to walk an arbitrary filesystem path upwards, it is recommended to first call Path.resolve() so as to resolve symlinks and eliminate ".." components.
sourceRefs.textQuote: If you want to walk an arbitrary filesystem path upwards, it is recommended to first call Path.resolve() so as to resolve symlinks and eliminate ".." components.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A string representing the final path component, excluding the drive and root, if any:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A string representing the final path component, excluding the drive and root, if any:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library/setup.py' ) . name 'setup.py'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library/setup.py' ) . name 'setup.py'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: UNC drive names are not considered:
sourceRefs.textQuote: UNC drive names are not considered:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//some/share/setup.py' ) . name 'setup.py' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//some/share' ) . name ''
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//some/share/setup.py' ) . name 'setup.py' >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//some/share' ) . name ''

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The last dot-separated portion of the final component, if any:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The last dot-separated portion of the final component, if any:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library/setup.py' ) . suffix '.py' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . suffix '.gz' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . suffix ''
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library/setup.py' ) . suffix '.py' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . suffix '.gz' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . suffix ''

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This is commonly called the file extension.
sourceRefs.textQuote: This is commonly called the file extension.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A list of the path’s suffixes, often called file extensions:
sourceRefs.textQuote: A list of the path’s suffixes, often called file extensions:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gar' ) . suffixes ['.tar', '.gar'] >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . suffixes ['.tar', '.gz'] >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . suffixes []
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gar' ) . suffixes ['.tar', '.gar'] >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . suffixes ['.tar', '.gz'] >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . suffixes []

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The final path component, without its suffix:
sourceRefs.textQuote: The final path component, without its suffix:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . stem 'library.tar' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar' ) . stem 'library' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . stem 'library'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar.gz' ) . stem 'library.tar' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library.tar' ) . stem 'library' >>> PurePosixPath ( 'my/library' ) . stem 'library'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Changed in version 3.14: A single dot (” . ”) is considered a valid suffix.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return a string representation of the path with forward slashes ( / ):
sourceRefs.textQuote: Return a string representation of the path with forward slashes ( / ):

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c: \\ windows' ) >>> str ( p ) 'c:\\windows' >>> p . as_posix () 'c:/windows'
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PureWindowsPath ( 'c: \\ windows' ) >>> str ( p ) 'c:\\windows' >>> p . as_posix () 'c:/windows'

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return whether the path is absolute or not. A path is considered absolute if it has both a root and (if the flavour allows) a drive:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Return whether the path is absolute or not. A path is considered absolute if it has both a root and (if the flavour allows) a drive:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> PurePosixPath ( '/a/b' ) . is_absolute () True >>> PurePosixPath ( 'a/b' ) . is_absolute () False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/a/b' ) . is_absolute () True >>> PureWindowsPath ( '/a/b' ) . is_absolute () False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:' ) . is_absolute () False >>> PureWindowsPath ( '//some/share' ) . is_absolute () True
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> PurePosixPath ( '/a/b' ) . is_absolute () True >>> PurePosixPath ( 'a/b' ) . is_absolute () False >>> PureWindowsPath ( 'c:/a/b' ) . is_absolute () True >>> PureWindowsPath ( '/a/b' ) . is_absolute () False >>> PureW

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Return whether or not this path is relative to the other path.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Return whether or not this path is relative to the other path.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc/passwd' ) >>> p . is_relative_to ( '/etc' ) True >>> p . is_relative_to ( '/usr' ) False
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> p = PurePath ( '/etc/passwd' ) >>> p . is_relative_to ( '/etc' ) True >>> p . is_relative_to ( '/usr' ) False

sourceRefs.fallbackText: This method is string-based; it neither accesses the filesystem nor treats “ .. ” segments specially. The following code is equivalent:
sourceRefs.textQuote: This method is string-based; it neither accesses the filesystem nor treats “ .. ” segments specially. The following code is equivalent:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: >>> u = PurePath ( '/usr' ) >>> u == p or u in p . parents False
sourceRefs.textQuote: >>> u = PurePath ( '/usr' ) >>> u == p or u in p . parents False

sourceRefs.textQuote: Added in version 3.9.
